results.possibleIdentity: Evelyn Rosana (sometimes spelled Rossana) was an artist active in the New York and Mexico City art scenes during the mid-to-late 1960s. In June 1964, she participated in a benefit art show and sale for the Militant Labor Forum in New York, exhibiting alongside notable avant-garde artists such as George Herms, Larry Zox, Basil King, and Dominick Capobianco. Several of her co-exhibitors from this period, including Zox and Herms, were later associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) circle or the E.A.T.-organized exhibition 'The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age' at MoMA in 1968. Additionally, in October 1964, she had a three-person exhibition at Galeria 1577 in Mexico City with artists Georges-Anne Roberts and Isadore Katz.

wikidata_candidates.label: Stephanie Evanitsky
wikidata_candidates.reasoning: The name 'Stephanie Evanitsky' is quite unique and matches exactly between both records. The Wikidata entity describes a choreographer, which fits the context of an artist, and the date 1970 on the index card aligns with the active professional career of someone of her stature.

results.possibleIdentity: Stephanie Evanitsky is a choreographer and pioneer of aerial dance, active primarily in the late 1960s and 1970s. She is best known for founding the Multigravitational Aerodance Group in 1969 (initially formed as the Aerodance Multigravitational Experiment Group), a company that explored movement in vertical space using specialized apparatuses like aluminum scaffolding, rope webs, trapezes, and elastic cords. Her work aimed to create the illusion of weightlessness and was heavily influenced by the multimedia experiments of Alwin Nikolais, with whom she studied at the Henry Street Settlement House after graduating from the Pratt Institute in 1966. Evanitsky's approach bridged the gap between dance and the visual arts; while her techniques were initially met with skepticism by the traditional dance community, they were highly receptive within the avant-garde and visual arts circles. She was awarded a Guggenheim Fellowship in the mid-1970s (sources cite 1974 or 1976) and presented work at the Guggenheim Museum. Her notable choreographic works include "Silver Scream Idols" (1971), "Splat" (1972), and "Buff Her Blind" (1974). Her association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is supported by archival records in the Jerome Robbins Papers at the New York Public Library, which group her work alongside E.A.T. and other experimental 20th-century performance organizations.

wikidata_candidates.label: Hera
wikidata_candidates.reasoning: The Wikidata entity Q123902098 refers to the artist commonly known as Hera, whose birth name is Betty Voelker. The dates (born 1940, active in 1970) and occupation (artist/sculptor) perfectly align with the card entry and provided context.

results.possibleIdentity: Betty Voelker (later known as Hera) was a conceptual and environmental artist active in the late 1960s and 1970s. Born as Betty Calvert in New Orleans, she worked professionally under the name Betty Voelker until approximately 1977. She is most recognized for her 'Tent Art,' which featured portable, collapsible structures and garments that expanded into enclosures. Notable works include 'Sweet Skirt' (1977), a construction of white taffeta and plastic flowers that could be entered, and 'Death Singer' (1977), both exhibited in the 'Locations' show at the Rose Art Museum, Brandeis University. In 1973, she exhibited 'Passageways' at the Fort Worth Art Center Museum. She was closely associated with the New York art scene of the 1970s, with materials related to her career held in the Political Art Documentation/Distribution (PAD/D) Archive at the Museum of Modern Art and the Giuseppe Panza papers at the Getty Research Institute. She also received a National Endowment for the Arts (NEA) grant for Expansion Arts in 1976. Some records indicate she served as an administrative figure or executive secretary for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Elizabeth 'Betty' Voelker (1931–2000) was an internationally known artist and colorist originally from Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. She received her B.F.A. in 1953 from the Carnegie Institute of Technology (now Carnegie Mellon University), where she studied under Samuel Rosenberg. Her work primarily consisted of abstract nature paintings and formal abstracted collages, such as 'Dominion' (1955) and 'Butoh'. She moved to San Francisco in 1958 and became active in the California art scene, lecturing at various museums and the University of California from 1972 to 1987. During her career, she received three grants from the Pollock-Krasner Foundation and residencies at the American Academy in Rome and the Djerassi Foundation. Her work is held in the collections of the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, the National Museum of American Art in Washington, D.C., and the Carnegie Museum of Art.

wikidata_candidates.label: Oliver Selfridge
wikidata_candidates.reasoning: The name Oliver Selfridge is quite distinct. While the Wikidata entity focuses on his career as an AI pioneer and mathematician at MIT, he was a known collaborator with artists and a participant in Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which matches the context of the card entry dated 1970.

results.possibleIdentity: Oliver Gordon Selfridge (1926–2008) was a pioneering mathematician and computer scientist who is often identified as the "Father of Machine Perception." Although primarily a scientist, he was deeply involved in the intellectual and creative circles that intersected with art and technology during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for his 1959 paper "Pandemonium: A Paradigm for Learning," which introduced a model of parallel processing using hierarchical "demons" to recognize patterns, a concept that significantly influenced cognitive psychology and artificial intelligence. Selfridge was a participant in the foundational 1956 Dartmouth Conference on AI and spent much of his career at MIT Lincoln Laboratory, Project MAC, Bolt Beranek and Newman (BBN), and GTE Laboratories. His artistic and creative output included several children's books such as "Fingers Come in Fives," "All About Mud," "Sticks," and "Trouble With Dragons." He is frequently cited in the context of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and cybernetic art (such as the 1968 "Cybernetic Serendipity" exhibition) due to his work's foundational role in machine learning and his personal collaborations with other pioneers of the "mechanical mind" era, like Marvin Minsky and Gordon Pask, who bridged the gap between science and the arts.
